quick_facts.label: What this role is
quick_facts.value: A Legal Officer in a public-sector undertaking (PSU) is an in-house legal executive role. Employer titles vary: recent official examples include POWERGRID “Officer Trainee (Law)” leading to Officer (Law), NTPC “Assistant Law Officers,” and NHPC “Trainee Officer (Law).”

quick_facts.label: Core qualification pattern
quick_facts.value: A law degree is the central entry qualification. For example, POWERGRID’s 2025 Officer Trainee (Law) notice required a full-time three-year LL.B. or five-year integrated law/LL.B. with at least 60% marks or equivalent CGPA; category relaxations and all other conditions were notice-specific.

quick_facts.label: CLAT-PG route
quick_facts.value: CLAT-PG can be used for PSU recruitment, but it is not a universal or permanent rule. POWERGRID’s 2025 notice used CLAT 2026 (PG) for Officer Trainee (Law), while NHPC used CLAT (PG)-2024 for Trainee Officer (Law) recruitment. Candidates should treat the score year, qualifying score, and selection stages in each employer advertisement as controlling.

quick_facts.label: AIBE relevance
quick_facts.value: AIBE is principally relevant to obtaining a Certificate of Practice for court practice after State Bar Council enrolment. It should not be assumed to be compulsory for every in-house PSU legal vacancy: POWERGRID’s cited entry notice specifies the law degree and CLAT-PG route, not AIBE, whereas the Bar Council of India describes AIBE as the route to a Certificate of Practice for advocates.

quick_facts.label: Posting context
quick_facts.value: PSU legal officers may be posted across a company’s offices, projects, stations, subsidiaries, or joint ventures. NHPC’s recruitment notice stated that placements could be at projects, power stations, and offices in India or abroad; exact mobility obligations depend on the employer’s notice and service rules.

quick_facts.label: Compensation context
quick_facts.value: Pay is employer-, grade-, location-, and date-specific. As a dated illustration, POWERGRID’s 2025 Officer Trainee (Law) notice stated basic pay of ₹40,000 during training and absorption as Officer (Law), E-2, on ₹50,000–₹1,60,000 IDA scale; it also stated approximate CTC of ₹11 lakh during training and ₹22.50 lakh after successful training, subject to location and terms.

sections.heading: What a PSU Legal Officer does
paragraphs: The precise portfolio depends on the PSU and grade, but the role is in-house legal work supporting the undertaking rather than a generic court-only role. A government power utility’s published legal-department duties include supervising litigation and providing legal advice on referred matters.

In a commercial PSU environment, legal work can also intersect with procurement and contract governance. NTPC’s published policy, for example, provides for legal-department vetting of certain orders relating to withholding business dealings under contracts, while NTPC also maintains a board committee to supervise legal matters. These examples support preparation for legal advice, dispute/litigation coordination, and review of legally significant contractual or business actions; the allocation of court appearances and external-counsel work varies by employer.
bullets: Provide legal opinions and research for operational, contractual, employment, regulatory, or governance questions referred by business units.
Support litigation and dispute-management processes, including coordination with counsel where the employer’s structure requires it.
Review or vet legal aspects of contracts, orders, and internal actions within delegated responsibilities.
Maintain rigorous records, drafting quality, confidentiality, and compliance with the PSU’s delegation, procurement, vigilance, and service frameworks.

sections.heading: Entry routes and recruitment process
paragraphs: The usual educational gateway is either a five-year integrated LL.B. after Class 12 or a three-year LL.B. after graduation. The exact accepted degree mode, marks threshold, age limit, reservation conditions, and whether final-year students may apply are determined by the individual recruitment notice. POWERGRID’s 2025 Officer Trainee (Law) notice is one concrete example: it required a full-time three-year LL.B. or five-year integrated law/LL.B. course with at least 60% marks or equivalent CGPA.

One important route is to sit CLAT-PG in the year specified by the PSU and separately apply on the employer’s careers portal. In the cited POWERGRID cycle, candidates were required to take CLAT 2026 (PG), then apply to POWERGRID; selection included the CLAT score, behavioural assessment, document verification, group discussion, interview, and pre-employment medical examination. The same notice gave 85% weight to CLAT marks, 3% to group discussion, and 12% to interview. These were POWERGRID’s 2025 terms, not a general formula for all PSUs.

Other PSUs may use a different CLAT-PG year and a different weighting. NHPC’s cited recruitment used CLAT (PG)-2024 for Trainee Officer (Law), shortlisting candidates for group discussion and personal interview, with 75% weight for the examination score and 25% for the combined group-discussion/interview score. Therefore, candidates should follow each live notification rather than relying on an old cutoff, prior scorecard, or another PSU’s selection design.

AIBE can be valuable where a career plan includes advocate enrolment and court practice, but it is not a substitute for the recruitment notice. The Bar Council of India states that a candidate who clears AIBE receives a Certificate of Practice and becomes eligible to practise in courts in India; a PSU may nevertheless recruit an in-house legal officer based on its stated degree, examination-score, experience, and selection requirements.
bullets: Monitor official careers pages of target PSUs and the official CLAT website; do not rely on coaching-site dates or cutoffs.
Read the full advertisement before registering: confirm accepted qualification, percentage calculation, age cut-off date, score year, reservation documents, service bond, and application route.
Keep law-degree marksheets, degree/provisional certificate, identity and category/PwBD documents, CLAT application details and scorecard, and employer-specific declarations ready for verification.
For litigation-oriented career options, separately assess State Bar Council enrolment and AIBE requirements.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: The role calls for strong legal analysis and drafting, but also for translating legal risk into practical advice for technical, commercial, finance, HR, procurement, and project teams. The published legal-department responsibilities at Delhi Transco and NTPC’s contract-governance material show the practical importance of litigation oversight, legal advice, and contract-related vetting.

Selection processes also test professional communication. POWERGRID’s cited recruitment included group discussion, behavioural assessment, and interview; candidates could appear for group discussion and interview in Hindi or English. This makes structured communication, fact-based reasoning, collaboration, and calm explanation of legal issues useful preparation areas alongside doctrinal law.
bullets: Legal research and concise, well-supported opinion writing.
Drafting and reviewing contracts, notices, internal notes, and case-related documents.
Civil procedure, arbitration/ADR, contract law, company law, labour and service law, administrative and regulatory law, and sector-specific rules relevant to a target PSU.
Matter management: tracking deadlines, documents, external counsel, approvals, and delegated authority.
Clear written and oral communication with non-lawyer stakeholders; Hindi/English ability may be useful where the employer permits either language in selection.

sections.heading: Workplace and employment conditions
paragraphs: This is typically an executive-cadre, in-house role in a government-owned enterprise. The work may be based at corporate offices, regional offices, project sites, power stations, or other operational locations. NHPC’s recruitment material explicitly contemplated placement at projects, power stations, offices, joint ventures, and subsidiary companies in different parts of India or abroad, illustrating why candidates should expect mobility when the advertisement says so.

Conditions can include a trainee period, medical fitness requirement, and a service agreement. For example, POWERGRID’s cited 2025 notice required selected Officer Trainee (Law) candidates to complete training and then serve at least three years, backed by a notice-specific service bond. These conditions are variable and should be checked afresh in every recruitment notice.
bullets: Work involves coordination with operational and corporate stakeholders, not only independent legal research.
Posting location can change according to organizational need and transfer policy.
Pre-employment medical examination, document verification, and employer-specific service bonds may be part of appointment.
Government/PSU employees applying to another PSU may need a no-objection certificate or application through the proper channel, where the notice requires it.

sections.heading: Progression and next moves
paragraphs: Career progression is employer-specific and governed by the PSU’s executive grading, performance processes, vacancy position, and promotion rules. A common early pattern is entry as a trainee followed by regularisation into an executive legal grade: POWERGRID’s 2025 notice described appointment as Officer Trainee (Law) and absorption after successful training as Officer (Law) at E-2 level.

With experience, a legal officer can seek broader portfolios in disputes, contracts, arbitration, employment/service matters, regulatory work, governance, or project and commercial legal support. Possible next labels—such as Senior Legal Officer, Legal Manager, or corporate counsel—are not uniform across PSUs, so candidates should view them as career directions rather than guaranteed promotions. A move into litigation practice also has separate professional requirements: the Bar Council of India identifies AIBE clearance as leading to a Certificate of Practice for practice in courts.
bullets: Build a documented record of reliable legal opinions, contract review, dispute support, and cross-functional work.
Develop sector knowledge in the PSU’s business—such as power, hydrocarbons, infrastructure, banking, insurance, transport, or manufacturing.
Consider LL.M. or focused continuing education where it strengthens a desired specialist portfolio; it is not automatically required for entry when the notice asks for LL.B./integrated law.
For a future litigation-advocate route, plan separately for State Bar Council enrolment and AIBE/Certificate of Practice requirements.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: PSU legal-officer remuneration is normally tied to the employer’s executive grade, pay revision framework, allowances, location, trainee status, and benefits policy. It should therefore be compared using the official notification for the exact post, not a generic online salary figure.

As dated context only, POWERGRID’s 2025 Officer Trainee (Law) notice stated ₹40,000 basic pay during training with IDA, HRA, and 12% perks on basic pay; after training it stated E-2 pay scale of ₹50,000–₹1,60,000 (IDA). It also listed approximate annual CTC of ₹11.00 lakh during training and ₹22.50 lakh after successful completion, expressly noting that CTC varied by posting location and terms. NHPC’s cited recruitment likewise placed Trainee Officer (Law) at E-2 ₹50,000–₹1,60,000 with allowances and benefits under company rules. Neither illustration predicts current or future pay at another PSU.
bullets: Compare basic pay, pay scale, trainee stipend/basic pay, DA/IDA, HRA or company accommodation, perks/cafeteria allowance, performance-related pay, medical cover, retirement benefits, and service-bond obligations.
Use only the dated official advertisement for salary and application-fee decisions; both terms and allowances can be revised.
Treat approximate CTC figures cautiously because they may include variable or location-dependent components.
